aliases: Korean marinated beef with steamed rice

english: Korean marinated beef with steamed rice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for beef bulgogi (FOODON_03302050) but does not have a specific combined bulgogi-with-rice entry; the rice component is not captured in this code. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Beef, marinated and grilled (bulgogi-type); rice component not separately coded in FoodEx2 at this level
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated combined bulgogi-with-rice composite code. A0FJP (beef, prepared) used as the closest defensible anchor. Rice component acknowledged as uncoded at this level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for Korean beef bulgogi with rice as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Korean beef bulgogi with rice is approximately 300 g, comprising roughly 150 g of steamed white rice and 150 g of marinated grilled beef with sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Korean Beef Bulgogi (with Rice) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Korean beef bulgogi (with rice), cr_id F05100065. All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 23572 (beef, ground, 85% lean, cooked) and USDA rice data as a blended composite approximation and documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Korean beef bulgogi with rice as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 72 is estimated using white rice (jasmine/short-grain) as the dominant carbohydrate source, which has a published GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The beef and marinade components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (72) × carbohydrate per serving (48.06 g for 300 g serving) / 100 = 34.6. This is a high-GL meal.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 174 mg/100 g from soy sauce marinade; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 522 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce in the marinade and avoid adding extra condiments such as gochujang or soy sauce at the table, as a full serving can contribute over 25% of the 2000 mg daily sodium target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice dominates carbohydrate content (16 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (0.71 g/100 g), producing a high glycaemic load of approximately 34.6 per standard serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce the glycaemic load, limit portion size to 150 g or less, and increase vegetable banchan side dishes to improve fibre intake and blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Red meat (beef) contributes saturated fat and haem iron; estimated saturated fat approximately 1.35 g/100 g; sodium 174 mg/100 g adds cardiovascular load at full serving sizes
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size of the beef component, choose lean cuts, and request reduced-sodium soy sauce to minimise combined saturated fat and sodium burden, consistent with AHA dietary guidelines for heart health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is a red meat high in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6) relevant to RA disease activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting beef with chicken or tofu bulgogi to reduce arachidonic acid load; garlic and ginger in the marinade provide modest anti-inflammatory polyphenol benefit and should be retained in any substitution.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Korean beef bulgogi with rice is a popular Korean dish consisting of thin-sliced beef marinated in a sweet-savoury sauce of soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, sugar, and grated pear or apple, then grilled or pan-fried and served over steamed white rice. The dish delivers a tender, slightly caramelised beef with umami depth from the soy marinade, balanced by the mild sweetness of the fruit-based tenderiser. Per 100 g, the dish provides 130 kcal, 7.58 g protein, 3.89 g fat, 16.02 g carbohydrate, 1.82 g sugar, 0.71 g dietary fibre, and 174 mg sodium. The dominant carbohydrate source is white rice, which gives the dish a high glycaemic index of approximately 72 and a high glycaemic load of approximately 34.6 per standard 300 g serving. Clinically, this dish is unsuitable for Type 2 Diabetes management without modification due to its high-GI white rice base and low fibre content. Sodium from the soy sauce marinade warrants caution for hypertension patients at full serving sizes. The red meat component raises concern for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis due to saturated fat and arachidonic acid content respectively. Halal-certified preparations are widely available. The dish is a staple of Korean cuisine and is commonly served in Korean restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia.

How many calories in bulgogi with rice?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100065) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central as a blended composite and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database using white rice as the dominant carbohydrate analogue, which is well-supported and clearly documented.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source for this entry): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this composite dish (F05100065) and are not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central for an exact Korean beef bulgogi with rice composite. Saturated_fat_g (1.35 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central blended composite of lean beef (NDB 23572) and cooked white rice proportions and is marked as adapted. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white rice (short-grain, cooked) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists in approved sources. FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology codes are approximate matches as no dedicated composite bulgogi-with-rice code exists in either system. Dietary tag 'low_calorie' from input retained as contextually plausible at 130 kcal/100 g but should be reviewed against Singapore HPB low-calorie meal criteria for formal labelling. Occasion tag 'snack' from input is not typical for this dish; meal_context updated to lunch/dinner.
